one_liner: A 2-year-old boy is brought in for evaluation of **short limbs with a relatively normal-sized trunk**, a large head with **frontal bossing**, and delayed motor milestones.

stage2.hpi_extension: Parents note that the child's arms and legs have always seemed short compared to his trunk and head. He began walking late (at 20 months) with a waddling gait. He has had several episodes of **otitis media** requiring antibiotics. No seizures. Cognitive development is on track: he speaks in short sentences and recognizes shapes and colors.
stage2.pmh: Full-term, uncomplicated delivery. Skeletal shortening noted on prenatal ultrasound at 30 weeks. Recurrent otitis media. No surgical interventions.
stage2.family_history: Both parents are of average height and healthy. Father is **47 years old** (advanced paternal age). No family history of skeletal dysplasia.
stage2.social_history: Only child. Lives with both parents. Daycare attendee.
stage2.physical_exam: Growth: **length well below 3rd percentile**, head circumference **above 97th percentile**, weight 50th percentile (disproportionate). HEENT: **large head with frontal bossing and midface hypoplasia**, depressed nasal bridge. Chest: normal. Extremities: **rhizomelic shortening** (proximal segments disproportionately short) of the arms and legs, **'trident hand' configuration** (separation between 3rd and 4th digits), genu varum (bowing of the legs). Spine: thoracolumbar kyphosis. Neuro: **hyperreflexia** (evaluate for foramen magnum stenosis), but normal strength and cognition.

lab_results.result: Targeted **FGFR3 gene sequencing**: heterozygous **Gly380Arg (G380R) mutation** in the transmembrane domain of **FGFR3** on chromosome 4p16.3.
lab_results.clinical_significance: **Activating (gain-of-function) mutation in FGFR3** confirms **achondroplasia**. Constitutive FGFR3 signaling inhibits chondrocyte proliferation at the epiphyseal growth plate, producing disproportionate short stature. Approximately 80% of cases are sporadic and associated with **advanced paternal age**.

lab_results.result: Skeletal survey: **short proximal (rhizomelic) long bones**, **narrow lumbar interpediculate distance** (progressive caudally, opposite of normal), **'champagne glass' pelvic inlet**, short sacrosciatic notch, small foramen magnum, square iliac wings. MRI craniocervical junction: mild **foramen magnum stenosis** without cord compression.
lab_results.clinical_significance: These radiographic features are classic for achondroplasia. **Foramen magnum stenosis** can produce central apnea or sudden infant death and is monitored clinically and radiographically.

stage5.final_clue: Management includes ENT follow-up for recurrent otitis media, serial polysomnography for obstructive and central sleep apnea, and yearly neurologic assessment. Parents are counseled that the patient has a 50% chance of transmitting the mutation to offspring, and that **homozygosity for achondroplasia is lethal in infancy** from thoracic cage restriction.

debrief.classic_presentation: Achondroplasia is the most common cause of **disproportionate dwarfism**. Classic features include **short limbs with a normal-sized trunk (rhizomelic shortening), a large head with frontal bossing, midface hypoplasia with a depressed nasal bridge, trident hand, genu varum, and lumbar lordosis**. Cognition and life expectancy are typically normal. Complications include **foramen magnum stenosis (risk of central apnea and sudden death in infancy), lumbar spinal stenosis, obstructive sleep apnea, and recurrent otitis media with conductive hearing loss**.
debrief.pathophysiology: Achondroplasia is an **autosomal dominant** disorder with **complete penetrance**, but approximately **80% of cases are sporadic**, arising as de novo mutations associated with **advanced paternal age**. It is caused by an **activating (gain-of-function) mutation in fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3)** on chromosome 4p16.3, most commonly the Gly380Arg substitution. Constitutive FGFR3 signaling **inhibits chondrocyte proliferation** at the epiphyseal growth plate, resulting in defective endochondral ossification while membranous ossification is preserved. **Homozygous achondroplasia is lethal in infancy**.
debrief.treatment_overview: Management is supportive and multidisciplinary: monitoring and surgical decompression of craniocervical or lumbar stenosis when indicated, pressure equalization tubes for chronic otitis media, polysomnography, and physical therapy. Limb-lengthening surgery is an option in selected patients. **Vosoritide** (a C-type natriuretic peptide analog) has been more recently approved in some regions to promote growth, but is not emphasized in First Aid 2017.
high_yield_pearls: Achondroplasia = **activating mutation in FGFR3** -> inhibits chondrocyte proliferation at the growth plate.
**Autosomal dominant** with full penetrance, but ~80% are sporadic (advanced paternal age).
Classic features: **rhizomelic short limbs, frontal bossing, midface hypoplasia, trident hand, lumbar lordosis, normal intelligence**.
**Homozygous achondroplasia is lethal**; heterozygotes have normal lifespan.
Watch for **foramen magnum stenosis** (central apnea/sudden death) and lumbar spinal stenosis in adults.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Achondroplasia' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Autosomal dominant diseases' (FGFR3).
